examples.title: Convolution Computation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to compute convolution of two discrete-time signals
examples.code:
// Discrete-time convolution: y[n] = x[n] * h[n]
// y[n] = Σ(k=-∞ to ∞) x[k] · h[n-k]

FUNCTION convolution(x[], h[], N, M):
 // x has N samples, h has M samples
 resultLength = N + M - 1
 y = ARRAY[resultLength] initialized to 0
 
 FOR n FROM 0 TO resultLength - 1:
 FOR k FROM 0 TO N-1:
 IF (n - k >= 0 AND n - k < M) THEN
 y[n] = y[n] + x[k] * h[n - k]
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 RETURN y

// Example usage:
x = [1, 2, 1] // Input signal
u = [1, 1, 1] // Unit step of length 3
y = convolution(x, u, 3, 3) // Result: [1, 3, 4, 3, 1]
examples.explanation: This algorithm implements linear convolution for discrete-time signals. It slides one signal (flipped) across the other, multiplies overlapping samples, and sums the products. Time complexity is O(N×M) where N and M are lengths of input signals.

examples.title: Signal Operations Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of basic signal operations: shifting, scaling, and reversal
examples.code:
// Signal Operations for discrete-time signals

// Time Shifting: y[n] = x[n-n0]
FUNCTION timeShift(x[], n0):
 // Right shift if n0 > 0, left if n0 < 0
 // For right shift, samples move to higher indices
 y = []
 IF n0 > 0: // Delay (right shift)
 y[0 to n0-1] = 0 // Zero padding at start
 y[n0 to end] = x[0 to end-n0]
 ELSE: // Advance (left shift)
 y = x[-n0 to end] // Remove first -n0 samples
 RETURN y

// Time Scaling: y[n] = x[n*L] or y[n] = x[n/L]
FUNCTION timeScale(x[], L, decimation):
 y = []
 IF decimation == TRUE: // y[n] = x[nL] (take every Lth sample)
 FOR n FROM 0 TO length(x)/L:
 y[n] = x[n * L]
 ELSE: // Expansion (zero insertion)
 FOR n FROM 0 TO length(x)-1:
 y[n*L] = x[n]
 // y[n*L+1] to y[n*L+L-1] = 0
 RETURN y

// Time Reversal: y[n] = x[-n]
FUNCTION timeReverse(x):
 y = REVERSE(x) // Flip the array
 RETURN y
examples.explanation: These are fundamental signal manipulation operations. Time shifting delays or advances a signal. Time scaling compresses (decimation) or expands (interpolation) the signal. Time reversal reflects the signal about the origin.

examples.title: Energy and Power Calculation
examples.description: Compute total energy and average power of discrete-time signals
examples.code:
// Energy: E = Σ|x[n]|²
// Power: P = lim(N→∞) (1/(2N+1)) Σ|x[n]|² from -N to N

FUNCTION calculateEnergy(x[]):
 energy = 0
 FOR n FROM 0 TO length(x)-1:
 energy = energy + x[n] * x[n] // x[n]²
 RETURN energy

FUNCTION calculatePower(x[], N):
 // For finite-length signal of length 2N+1
 sumSquares = 0
 FOR n FROM 0 TO 2*N:
 sumSquares = sumSquares + x[n] * x[n]
 power = sumSquares / (2*N + 1)
 RETURN power

// Determine signal type
FUNCTION classifySignal(x[]):
 E = calculateEnergy(x)
 P = calculatePower(x, length(x)/2)
 
 IF E > 0 AND P == 0:
 RETURN "Energy Signal"
 ELSE IF P > 0 AND E == INFINITY:
 RETURN "Power Signal"
 ELSE IF E == 0 AND P == 0:
 RETURN "Neither Energy nor Power"
 ELSE:
 RETURN "Invalid (cannot be both)"
examples.explanation: Energy signals have finite total energy (E > 0, P = 0). Power signals have infinite energy but finite non-zero power (E = ∞, P > 0). This code helps classify signals based on these criteria.
